findings.title: Contract artifact denies API changes that the plan explicitly makes
findings.description: The contracts README says this change does not introduce or modify HTTP API endpoints, but the proposal, tasks, and work packages add an `agent_requirements` field to `/work/submit` and new filtering semantics to `/work/claim`. Those are observable request/behavior changes to existing interfaces. Treating them as already covered by the existing contract leaves no canonical artifact to update or validate for the new field and claim behavior.
findings.suggestion: Mark the `/work/*` additions as additive API contract changes, identify the canonical contract artifact that must be updated, and add explicit tasks/tests for the new request field and claim semantics.

findings.title: No source of truth for an agent's supported archetypes
findings.description: The spec requires `claim()` to compare task requirements against the claiming agent's archetype compatibility, but phase 3 only adds `agent_requirements` to tasks and says claim should pass the agent's archetype capabilities. No task extends `agents.yaml`, `register_session`, `agent_sessions`, or discovery metadata to actually declare and persist supported archetypes. In the current codebase the coordinator only knows generic `capabilities`, so routing cannot be implemented deterministically from the planned changes.
findings.suggestion: Add a concrete compatibility path: define where supported archetypes live, persist them in agent/session state, and update `claim_task()` plus tests to use that field for routing.

findings.title: Complexity escalation depends on metadata the schema never defines
findings.description: The spec and design require escalation on `complexity: high`, but phase 3 only adds an `archetype` field to `work-packages.schema.json`. The current work-packages schema metadata block has no `complexity` field, and no task updates planning or validation to emit and validate it. One of the four mandated escalation triggers therefore cannot ever fire.
findings.suggestion: Add a schema/task change for `metadata.complexity` with a bounded enum and corresponding validation tests, or remove the `complexity: high` trigger from the proposal, design, and spec.

findings.title: min_trust_level is declared but never enforced in claim routing
findings.description: The spec defines `agent_requirements.min_trust_level`, and task 3.1.1 adds it to the dataclass, but the implementation tasks only describe archetype filtering. Task 3.1.3 and migration 3.5.1 never require `claim()` or `claim_task()` to compare the claimant's resolved trust level against the requested minimum. That omission would allow lower-trust agents to claim restricted work.
findings.suggestion: Extend phase 3 so `claim()` resolves the agent trust level and `claim_task()` rejects tasks whose `agent_requirements.min_trust_level` exceeds it, with positive and negative tests at the service and HTTP/MCP layers.

findings.title: Phase 2 has no concrete execution hook that turns archetypes into real Task behavior
findings.description: The design says skills will write `Task(archetype="...")` and that dispatch-time logic will compose prompts and resolve models, but the planned file edits only touch skill markdown, `agents_config.py`, and a review-dispatcher fallback path. No package identifies the runtime layer that intercepts ordinary skill `Task()` calls for sequential and local-parallel execution and rewrites them into supported `model=` plus composed prompt behavior. In the current repo there is no concrete implementation surface for generic `Task(archetype=...)` resolution.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit runtime integration task and owner for ordinary skill dispatch, or narrow phase 2 so skills continue using `model=` until the underlying Task execution layer natively supports archetypes.

findings.title: agent_requirements storage is left ambiguous across the phase
findings.description: Task 3.1.2 says to persist `agent_requirements` via `input_data` or a new column, but task 3.5.1 later requires a dedicated JSONB column and RPC filtering. Leaving the storage model undecided makes the SQL/RPC design ambiguous, weakens package boundaries, and undermines the claim that each task can be implemented cleanly in one commit.
findings.suggestion: Choose one storage model up front, preferably the dedicated JSONB column referenced by 3.5.1, and update all phase 3 tasks and work packages to use that decision consistently.

findings.title: Verification steps do not prove the behaviors the spec requires
findings.description: The phase 1 and phase 2 skill packages verify changes with `grep -c 'model='` and `grep -c 'archetype='`, which only count matches somewhere in the files. They do not prove that every `Task()` call is annotated, that each skill uses the correct mapping, or that failures report file and line numbers as required by the spec. Likewise `wp-queue-routing` validates the template work-packages file, which does not exercise the new `archetype` field or the invalid-name rejection scenario.
findings.suggestion: Replace the grep-based verification with the parser-based pytest checks promised in tasks 1.0.1 and 3.0.2, using fixtures that cover complete annotation, correct mapping, and invalid archetype values.

findings.title: wp-queue-routing claims API endpoint locks outside its declared write scope
findings.description: `wp-queue-routing` only writes `work_queue.py`, migrations, tests, and `work-packages.schema.json`, but it reserves `api:POST /work/submit` and `api:POST /work/claim` lock keys. Those endpoints are actually modified in `wp-api-mcp-integration`, which claims the same locks later. This over-serializes the DAG and makes the lock model inconsistent with declared file ownership.
findings.suggestion: Remove the `/work/*` API lock keys from `wp-queue-routing`, or move the endpoint changes into the same package so the lock keys match actual ownership.

findings.title: Dependency escalation threshold is internally inconsistent
findings.description: The requirement text says escalation triggers for `depends on 2+ other packages`, but the logged reason and design algorithm use `>2 packages`. The scenario at lines 150-153 uses two dependencies and still expects the `>2` message, so implementers and test authors can derive different thresholds from the same artifact set.
findings.suggestion: Make the threshold consistent across requirement text, scenarios, log messages, design pseudocode, and tests: either use `>=2` everywhere or change the scenario to three dependencies.
